messages.content: You are a practical personal finance explainer. Provide neutral, actionable instructions without endorsing specific apps or banks. Stay on-topic and avoid unrelated public-service announcements, pop-culture mentions, or historical asides. Use simple math and clear steps.
Walk me through setting up a 50/30/20 budget from scratch. Include a small worked example with monthly take-home pay of $3,600, a simple spreadsheet layout, and what to do if my income is irregular.

Here’s a straightforward way to set up a 50/30/20 budget and keep it running.

What 50/30/20 means
- 50% Needs: essential bills and minimum debt payments (rent, utilities, basic groceries, transportation, insurance, required prescriptions).
- 30% Wants: non-essentials (dining out, entertainment, subscriptions, hobbies, travel).
- 20% Savings/Debt payoff: emergency fund, retirement contributions, extra debt payments, sinking funds for future expenses.

Worked example with $3,600 take-home
- Needs (50%): $1,800
- Wants (30%): $1,080
- Savings/Debt (20%): $720

Simple spreadsheet layout (one sheet)
- Columns: Category | Type (Need/Want/Savings) | Budgeted | Actual | Difference | Notes
- Rows (sample):
  - Rent | Need | 1,200 |     |     |
  - Utilities | Need | 150 |     |     |
  - Groceries (basic) | Need | 300 |     |     |
  - Transit/Gas | Need | 150 |     |     |
  - Insurance | Need | 0–200 (your value) |     |     |
  - Dining Out | Want | 200 |     |     |
  - Streaming | Want | 30 |     |     |
  - Entertainment | Want | 150 |     |     |
  - Misc Wants | Want | 700 (adjust to hit 30%) |     |     |
  - Emergency Fund | Savings | 300 |     |     |
  - Retirement/IRA | Savings | 200 |     |     |
  - Extra Debt Payment | Savings/Debt | 220 |     |     |
- Bottom rows: Totals for Needs, Wants, Savings should sum to $3,600 and match 50/30/20.

Setup steps
1) List net income: Add up all take-home pay for the month (after tax and deductions).
2) List fixed bills: Rent, insurance, subscriptions; put them under Needs or Wants.
3) Estimate variable categories: Groceries, gas, dining, entertainment. Start modest; you can adjust later.
4) Allocate to match 50/30/20: Move dollars until Needs ≈50%, Wants ≈30%, Savings/Debt ≈20%.
5) Pick payment dates: Align savings and extra debt payments to happen right after each paycheck so you don’t spend them first.
6) Track Actual: Update weekly. If a category is running hot, trim Wants first.
7) Month-end reset: Compare Budgeted vs Actual, note what went off track, and adjust next month’s numbers.

Irregular income approach
- Find your monthly “floor”: Average your last 6–12 months of take-home and pick a conservative amount you can usually count on.
- Build a buffer: Keep one month of expenses in a separate buffer account. When income is above the floor, top up the buffer; when below, draw from it.
- Create a priority list for Savings/Debt: Rank items (1) minimum debt payments, (2) essentials, (3) emergency fund, (4) extra debt, (5) other goals. Fully fund higher priorities before moving to lower ones in lean months.
- Use percentage-based allocations on payday: Apply 50/30/20 to whatever arrives. Example: If $1,200 comes in, set aside roughly $600 Needs, $360 Wants, $240 Savings/Debt immediately.

Tuning tips
- If Needs exceed 50%: Look for reductions (housing with roommates, cheaper plans) or temporarily pull from Wants while you work the number down.
- If debt is high: Keep Needs at 50%, trim Wants below 30%, and push more than 20% to debt payoff until it’s manageable.
- Automate what you can: Automatic transfers to savings right after payday reduce friction.

Signs it’s working
- You’re consistently saving something every month.
- Bills are paid on time without scrambling.
- Over a few cycles, categories fit your real life without frequent overshoots.

This structure gives you a clear plan on day one and a feedback loop to refine it month by month.
